title: Peace narrative, market profits — the insider trading the SEC isn't investigating
narrative: Trump administration claims it is "ending wars" and securing peace deals. White House briefings credit the president with "ending wars, boosting alliances." Stock markets rallied strongly in the week of May 11-15, with the Dow entering correction territory. Fortune ran: "Wall Street has decided the war is already over."
telemetry: Guardian market analysis (May 14): equities fell sharply on war escalation news, rallied on peace talk headlines — but the actual Iran conflict has not ended
BBC reported a recurring pattern of suspicious trading ahead of major war announcements
Yahoo Finance flagged 'insider trading suspicion' tied to Trump administration Iran war communications
Dow briefly entered correction territory in early May as oil prices climbed — yet by mid-May was pricing in peace the administration has not delivered
An administration that 'ended wars' is simultaneously presiding over an active conflict and profiting politically from a market narrative that does not match facts on the ground
analysis: 'Peace Narrative, War Profits.' The BBC and Yahoo Finance flagged suspicious trading ahead of war announcements. That's not a coincidence — that's a signal. Someone with access to administration Iran policy communications was positioning markets before the announcements hit the news. The SEC hasn't commented. When the people who are supposed to investigate this kind of thing stay quiet, the story becomes what was found, not what was traded.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Markets moved before peace announcements. The SEC hasn't opened an inquiry. When the regulator responsible for exactly this kind of market manipulation stays silent, the question isn't whether it happened — it's who benefits.
